results.title: Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions
results.content: !(data:image/png;base64, [...] Programming has historically been about writing explicit rules in a formal language designed for the purpose, a programming language. A function is defined by code, and the computer executes it deterministically. For many tasks, this paradigm works beautifully: sorting numbers, processing structured data, computing matrix products. Yet a large class of real-world functions resists precise specification. Consider, for instance, filtering a computer log to alert someone only on the log lines or messages that matter, repairing malformed JSON, or ranking search results by intent. Even apparently “simple” tasks, such as writing a regular expression to parse text with many edge cases, prove brittle. Beyond underspecification, real-world inputs are noisy: typos and format drift routinely break [...] but achieves it via per-task compile rather than per-task fine-tuning.

results.title: Program-as-Weights compiles LLM tasks into a 0.6B adapter | AI Weekly
results.content:
Originally reported by
paper

Original headline:
Program-as-Weights: 0.6B Model Matches Qwen3-32B at 1/50th Memory by Compiling LLM Functions Into Neural Adapters

3x/week. Breaking AI alerts only when they matter. Free, no spam, opt out anytime.

©2015-2026 AI News Weekly | Latest Issue | Archives | Learning AI

Log in | Unsubscribe [...] The honest caveat is that the parity claim is measured on the authors' own FuzzyBench distribution, and the paper is a single-lab arXiv preprint from July 2026 rather than a reproduced result. What the abstract does not give you is how much the compile step costs per function, which task categories the 0.6B interpreter starts to fall behind on, or how this actually compares against just LoRA fine-tuning the same small model. Take the specifics as reported, not settled. Still, the direction, treating foundation models as tool builders rather than per-input solvers, is the part worth watching.

AI Weekly tracks stories like this 3x/week. Breaking alerts only when they matter. 44,000+ AI professionals.

Originally reported by
paper [...] The paper, titled Program-as-Weights, reports that a 4B compiler emits a parameter-efficient adapter for a frozen 0.6B Qwen3 interpreter, and that the compiled function matches direct Qwen3-32B prompting on the authors' benchmarks while using roughly one-fiftieth of the inference memory. They also report about 30 tokens per second on a MacBook M3, which is the number that makes the paper feel less like a lab curiosity. The compiler itself was trained on FuzzyBench, a new 10-million-example dataset the authors are releasing alongside the method.

# Weights & Measures Complaints

## Filing a Complaint

Were you overcharged for something you bought? Did you use a scale, pump, or other device that felt inaccurate? If so, you can file a complaint that will be investigated by a County Weights & Measures inspector. Please fill out one of the complaint forms below with as much detail as you can and email it to wm.awm@sdcounty.ca.gov so that it can be processed as efficiently as possible.

General Complaint

For complaints about inaccurate prices or devices such as gas pumps, scales, taxis, cash registers, etc.

Submeter Complaint

For complaints about your water, gas, or electric utility submeter.

## Required Information [...] File a complaint if a scale or measuring device is being used improperly, or if a business isn’t charging by the correct weight or measurement. Be sure to include the scale location in the store.
 For scale complaints, use the general complaint form.

### Submeters

Water, gas, or electric utility submeters are devices sometimes installed on rental properties such as apartments or mobile homes to measure utility use by an individual unit. The submeter allows the landlord or property manager to track and bill each unit or tenant based on usage rather than splitting the total bill among all units. [...] - Small Business Support

# ProgramAsWeights

results.title: Weight-Loss Startup Calibrate Faces Mountain of Patient Complaints and Refunds - Business Insider
results.content:
Patients became frustrated because they were kept out of the loop while the team sought their insurance companies' approval for a prescription, five former employees said. When Calibrate did respond to members during this process, it often sent a generic message, because the employees who responded to members' questions had little insight into the status of prescriptions, two of the former employees said.

"It's not someone buying a mattress that can wait two weeks for a response to their message — it's someone who's having side effects. They need a response right away," the current employee said. "They deserve a response, they've paid for a program." Calibrate leaders "just don't wanna prioritize that," she continued. [...] Kenyon also acknowledged that Calibrate patients were fed up with the time it takes staff to respond to their messages, and she said the startup is working to be more communicative.

"That's the piece that's really frustrating for members, and it's unacceptable, and we commit to fix it," she said.

She sent a mass email to members on August 21, the day of Insider's scheduled interview with her, apologizing for recent disruptions in access to medication and promising to start responding to members more quickly by the end of September. [...] Reflecting these problems, the app, launched in June, now has an average rating of 1.1 out of five stars in the Apple App Store.

A current employee said a round of layoffs in April might have contributed to the bungled app launch, since Calibrate had only "a skeleton crew" of engineers to fix the app's issues. Kenyon wrote in her statement to Insider that this characterization was inaccurate.

Kenyon said in the interview that the process of moving members and Calibrate staff from previous software to the new app's platform was "exceptionally broken," and cut off some patients' access to the app entirely for a period of time. She said the issues have been fixed.
